quiz.title: Unit 9, Quiz 5: Inflation, Wages, and Monetary Dynamics
quiz.subtitle: GDP Effects, Inflation Types, Phillips Curve, and Real vs. Nominal Concepts
quiz.subject: AP Macroeconomics
quiz.topic: GDP Components, Inflation Classification, Money Supply, Real Wages, Phillips Curve, Exchange Rates

questions.question: An economy reallocates scarce resources toward investment, increasing I by $70 billion but reducing C by $50 billion. What is the immediate effect on GDP?
questions.explanation: GDP = C + I + G + (X − M). With G, X, and M unchanged, ΔGDP = ΔC + ΔI = (−$50B) + (+$70B) = +$20 billion. The increase in investment outweighs the decrease in consumption.

questions.question: An increase in injections in the economy may lead to:
options: An outward shift of aggregate demand and demand pull inflation
An outward shift of aggregate demand and cost push inflation
An outward shift of aggregate supply and demand pull inflation
An outward shift of aggregate supply and cost push inflation
questions.explanation: Injections (investment, government spending, exports) directly increase aggregate demand, shifting the AD curve outward. If the economy is near or at full capacity, this increased demand can lead to demand-pull inflation. Cost-push inflation (B) comes from supply-side factors, not demand. Options C and D involve AS shifts, which are not directly caused by increased injections.

questions.question: What is inflation below 10 percent per year known as?
questions.explanation: Inflation below 10 percent per year is commonly referred to as creeping inflation. It is a moderate and manageable rate of inflation. Hyperinflation (A) is extremely high (often >50% per month). Disinflation (B) refers to a decrease in the rate of inflation, not a specific rate. Galloping inflation (C) is very high (10–1000%).

questions.question: What is it known as when inflation is about 20% per year?
questions.explanation: Inflation around 20% per year is typically classified as galloping inflation. It is severe enough to disrupt economic stability but not yet hyperinflation. Creeping inflation (A) is below 10%. Walking inflation (B) is moderate (around 3–10%). Hyperinflation (D) is extremely high, often exceeding 50% per month.

questions.question: If every company increases the wages of its workers in the same period, given constant technology, which of the following is TRUE?
options: This would improve the standard of living of the workers
This would make no difference to the workers
This would reduce the standard of living of the workers
Any of the three options is possible
questions.explanation: With constant technology and all companies raising wages simultaneously, production costs rise uniformly. Firms pass these costs to consumers through higher prices. Nominal wages rise, but real wages (purchasing power) remain unchanged because the price level increases proportionally. Therefore, there is no real improvement — the standard of living stays the same.

questions.question: If the money supply grows by 1% each year, inflation would grow in the long run by ___________
questions.explanation: According to the quantity theory of money, in the long run, the inflation rate equals the growth rate of the money supply minus the growth rate of real output. If real output is constant, a 1% increase in money supply leads to 1% inflation. If real output grows, inflation would be less than 1%. The question assumes long-run equilibrium, so the most direct answer is 1%.

questions.question: When the rate of inflation is greater than the rate of growth of money, the real money supply ____________
options: could do any of the above
questions.explanation: The real money supply is nominal money supply adjusted for inflation (M/P). If inflation (rise in P) exceeds the growth rate of nominal money (M), then M/P falls — the real money supply decreases. People's money holdings lose purchasing power faster than new money is created.

questions.question: The effects of inflation on the price competitiveness of a country’s products may be offset by:
questions.explanation: Inflation makes a country's exports more expensive, reducing competitiveness. A depreciation of the currency offsets this by making exports cheaper for foreign buyers. Appreciation (A) would worsen competitiveness. Revaluation (B) is similar to appreciation in a fixed exchange rate system. Lower inflation overseas (D) would further reduce competitiveness, not offset it.

questions.question: In the short run, unemployment may fall below the natural rate of unemployment if:
options: Nominal wages have risen less than inflation
Nominal wages have risen at the same rate as inflation
Nominal wages have risen more than inflation
Nominal wages have risen less than unemployment
questions.explanation: If nominal wages rise less than inflation, real wages fall. This makes labor cheaper for firms, encouraging them to hire more workers, which can temporarily push unemployment below the natural rate. This aligns with the Phillips curve concept where unexpected inflation reduces real wages and boosts employment in the short run.

questions.question: According to the Phillips curve, unemployment will return to the natural rate when:
options: Nominal wages are equal to expected wages
Real wages are back at equilibrium level
Nominal wages are growing faster than inflation
Inflation is higher than the growth of nominal wages
questions.explanation: In the Phillips curve framework, unemployment returns to the natural rate when real wages adjust back to their equilibrium level. This occurs after workers fully incorporate inflation expectations into wage demands, restoring the real wage that clears the labor market. Options A, C, and D describe nominal relationships, but it is the restoration of real wage equilibrium that brings unemployment back to its natural rate.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent understanding of inflation dynamics, wage-price relationships, and Phillips curve mechanics.
scoreMessages.70: Good grasp; review the distinction between nominal and real concepts and the quantity theory of money.
scoreMessages.50: Needs focus on inflation classification, real vs. nominal wages, and the relationship between money supply and inflation.
scoreMessages.0: Revisit: GDP components, inflation types, real money supply, and Phillips curve adjustments.
